slides.title: Errors Happen — Don't Crash
slides.body: When Python hits a problem it can't solve, it raises an **exception** and your program stops cold. Learning to *catch* exceptions means your program can recover instead of crashing.
slides.tip: Every exception has a name — like `ValueError` or `KeyError` — that tells you what went wrong.

slides.title: try / except: Your Safety Net
slides.body: Wrap **risky code** in a `try` block, then handle the failure in `except`. If no error occurs, the `except` block is skipped entirely.

slides.title: Name the Exception You Expect
slides.body: Always catch a **specific** exception type — never use a bare `except:`. Catching everything hides real bugs that you didn't intend to handle.

slides.title: Inspect the Error with `as e`
slides.body: Add `as e` to capture the exception object and **see exactly what Python was complaining about** — great for debugging or showing users a helpful message.
slides.output: Caught: invalid literal for int() with base 10: 'abc'

slides.title: else and finally: Full Control
slides.body: `else` runs only when **no exception** occurred. `finally` runs **no matter what** — perfect for cleanup like closing files.

slides.title: You're Exception-Proof Now
slides.body: You can catch errors with `try/except`, name specific exception types, inspect messages with `as e`, and guarantee cleanup with `finally`. Your programs no longer have to crash — they can *recover*.
slides.tip: When in doubt, print the error with `as e` so you always know what actually went wrong.
